text: The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a peritoneal flap, exposing the underlying anatomical structures for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon develops the peritoneal flap and proceeds with a lateral first dissection due to the presence of a retro rectus reservoir, deviating from the usual medial first approach.
The surgeon begins the dissection in the pre-transversalis plane, later transitioning to re-establish a pre-peritoneal dissection, revealing the anatomical layers involved in the procedure.
The surgeon works towards the indirect hernia sac, demonstrating the reduction of a moderate-sized inguinal scrotal hernia.
The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels medially and develops a larger lateral pocket to accommodate an extra-large mesh during the hernia repair.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection, encountering scarring around the reservoir that appears to be adhered to the transversalis fascia.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the initial scarring layer surrounding the encapsulated plastic reservoir, revealing its structure beneath the tissue.
The surgeon begins the retropubic dissection, carefully maneuvering around the obturator and the vas deferens.

The surgeon establishes a critical view of safety for the myopectenial orifice, revealing anatomical landmarks necessary for safe dissection. The focus then shifts to retropubic dissection, continuing the surgical procedure.

The reservoir is visualized outside of its initial capsule, having exited over the pubis. The dissection extends well beyond the midline, with further development of the contralateral dissection. The anatomy of the midline is reviewed.
The surgeon visualizes the reservoir tubing alongside Cooper's ligament, femoral space, indirect space, and surrounding vessels. The mesh is positioned without removing the reservoir.
The surgeon augments the extra-large heavyweight mesh by creating a two-centimeter slit to ensure coverage over the contralateral side.
The surgeon places a mid-weight mesh to address the indirect problem, ensuring appropriate coverage over the neurovascular plane of the obturator (NPO).
The surgeon secures the mesh to the scar tissue, avoiding attachment to Cooper's ligament, ensuring stable placement within the surgical field.
